{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}} {{more footnotes|date=February 2021}} '''''Setsuwa''''' ({{langx|ja|説話|setsu wa}}) is a Japanese literary genre. It consists of [[myth]]s, [[legend]]s, [[Folklore|folktales]], and [[anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[otogizōshi]]'' genre.

''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[Tendai]] priest [[Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = McCullough | first = Helen Craig | authorlink = Helen Craig McCullough | title = Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology | publisher = Stanford University Press | year = 1990 | isbn = 0-8047-1960-8 }} * {{NKBD}}]]

''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[Tendai]] priest [[Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}} {{more footnotes|date=February 2021}} '''''Setsuwa''''' ({{langx|ja|説話|setsu wa}}) is a Japanese literary genre. It consists of [[myth]]s, [[legend]]s, [[Folklore|folktales]], and [[anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[Tendai]] priest [[Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

It consists of [[myth]]s, [[legend]]s, [[Folklore|folktales]], and [[anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[Tendai]] priest [[Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Japanese folktales]]

''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[Tendai]] priest [[Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese folktales|Japanese folktales]]

{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}} {{more footnotes|date=February 2021}} ''''Setsuwa''''' ({{langx|ja|説話|setsu wa}}) is a Japanese literary genre. It consists of [[myth]]s, [[legend]]s, [[:en:Folklore|folktales]], and [[:en:Anecdote|Anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>

''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.

''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.

Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

It consists of [[:en:Myth|Myth]]s, [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]]s, [[:en:Folklore|folktales]], and [[:en:Anecdote|Anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>

However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"

Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Setsuwa]][[en:Setsuwa]][[fr:Setsuwa]][[hu:Szecuva]][[it:Setsuwa]][[ja:説話]][[ko:설화]][[pl:Setsuwa]][[ru:Сэцува]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1062383]] Wikidata name: setsuwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese literary genre}} {{more footnotes|date=February 2021}} ''''Setsuwa''''' ({{langx|ja|説話|setsu wa}}) is a Japanese literary genre. It consists of [[:en:Myth|Myth]]s, [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]]s, [[:en:Folklore|folktales]], and [[:en:Anecdote|Anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[:en:Oral tradition|Oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese folktales|Japanese folktales]]

Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

It consists of [[:en:Myth|Myth]]s, [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]]s, [[:en:Folklore|folktales]], and [[:en:Anecdote|Anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[:en:Oral tradition|Oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.

''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

It consists of [[:en:Myth|Myth]]s, [[:en:en:Legend|en:Legend]]s, [[:en:Folklore|folktales]], and [[:en:Anecdote|Anecdote]]s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on [[:en:Oral tradition|Oral tradition]] and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''[[:en:Konjaku Monogatarishū|Konjaku Monogatarishū]]'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

The [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] priest [[:en:Keisei (monk)|Keisei]] wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese folktales|Japanese folktales]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Setsuwa]][[en:Setsuwa]][[fr:Setsuwa]][[hu:Szecuva]][[it:Setsuwa]][[ja:説話]][[ko:설화]][[pl:Setsuwa]][[ru:Сэцува]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Setsuwa]][[en:Setsuwa]][[fr:Setsuwa]][[hu:Szecuva]][[it:Setsuwa]][[ja:説話]][[ko:설화]][[pl:Setsuwa]][[ru:Сэцува]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Setsuwa]][[en:Setsuwa]][[fr:Setsuwa]][[hu:Szecuva]][[it:Setsuwa]][[ja:説話]][[ko:설화]][[pl:Setsuwa]][[ru:Сэцува]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1062383]] Wikidata name: setsuwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese literary genre}} {{more footnotes|date=February 2021}} ''''Setsuwa''''' ({{langx|ja|説話|setsu wa}}) is a Japanese literary genre. It consists of {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth}}s, {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend}}s, {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folktales}}, and {{ill|Anecdote|en|Anecdote|WD=Q193206|lt=Anecdote}}s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on {{ill|Oral tradition|en|Oral tradition|WD=Q209815|lt=Oral tradition}} and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatarishū}}'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} priest {{ill|Keisei (monk)|en|Keisei (monk)|WD=Q11495164|lt=Keisei}} wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese folktales|en|Japanese folktales|WD=Q2349495|lt=Japanese folktales}}
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = McCullough | first = Helen Craig | authorlink = Helen Craig McCullough | title = Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology | publisher = Stanford University Press | year = 1990 | isbn = 0-8047-1960-8 }} * {{NKBD}}]]

''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatarishū}}'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} priest {{ill|Keisei (monk)|en|Keisei (monk)|WD=Q11495164|lt=Keisei}} wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

It consists of {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth}}s, {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend}}s, {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folktales}}, and {{ill|Anecdote|en|Anecdote|WD=Q193206|lt=Anecdote}}s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on {{ill|Oral tradition|en|Oral tradition|WD=Q209815|lt=Oral tradition}} and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.
==Notable examples== The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'', shortened from the full title ''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki'' (A Chronicle of Wondrous Tales of Good and Evil in Japan), is a collection of ''setsuwa'' tales focused entirely on Buddhist themes, and is the oldest known collection of Buddhist legends in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatarishū}}'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} priest {{ill|Keisei (monk)|en|Keisei (monk)|WD=Q11495164|lt=Keisei}} wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>

''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatarishū|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū}}'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} priest {{ill|Keisei (monk)|en|Keisei (monk)|WD=Q11495164|lt=Keisei|1=Keisei (monk)}} wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese folktales|en|Japanese folktales|WD=Q2349495|lt=Japanese folktales|1=Japanese folktales}}

{{Short description|Japanese literary genre}} {{more footnotes|date=February 2021}} ''''Setsuwa''''' ({{langx|ja|説話|setsu wa}}) is a Japanese literary genre. It consists of {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth|1=Myth}}s, {{ill|en:Legend|en|en:Legend|WD=Q44342|lt=en:Legend|1=Legend}}s, {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|WD=Q36192|lt=folktales|1=Folklore}}, and {{ill|Anecdote|en|Anecdote|WD=Q193206|lt=Anecdote|1=Anecdote}}s. Among the {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}}, those that are full-length are generally referred to as {{transliteration|ja|monogatari}}. In Japan, the term {{transliteration|ja|setsuwa}} is also applied to similar works around the world.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230512185200/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AA%AC%E8%A9%B1-548546|script-title=ja:Setsuwa|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|archive-date=12 May 2023|access-date=12 May 2023}}</ref>
''Setsuwa'' means "spoken story". As one of the vaguest forms of literature, ''setsuwa'' is believed to have been passed down or presented in the form of narrations. ''Setsuwa'' are based foremost on {{ill|Oral tradition|en|Oral tradition|WD=Q209815|lt=Oral tradition|1=Oral tradition}} and existed primarily as folktales or in other non-written forms before being recorded and committed to text. However, some writers question whether all ''setsuwa'' tales were originally oral tradition, or only mostly so. Although there are no formal rules regarding what constitutes ''setsuwa'' as a genre, stories in the ''setsuwa'' style "have in common brevity; an uncomplicated plot unfolded in plain, direct language; character delineation through dialogue and action rather than through description and psychological analysis; and a predilection for amusing, startling, dramatic, or marvelous subject matter.<ref>{{Cite book |last=McCullough |first=Hellen Craig |title=Classical Japanese Prose: An Anthology |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1990 |pages=7 |language=en-us}}</ref>"
''Setsuwa'' vary quite considerably in topic, but can be divided into two main groups: general and Buddhist. Buddhist ''setsuwa'' often contain themes of karmic retribution or miracles, while the content of “general” ''setsuwa'' is largely either secular in nature or focused on traditional Japanese religion and spirituality such as Shinto.
Many ''setsuwa'' collections were compiled during the Heian and Kamakura periods (8th-12th centuries, 12th-14th centuries). These collections were often assembled by Buddhist monks, but the authorship of many such works is still unknown or heavily debated.
Setsuwa may be found integrated in other literature or in ''setsuwa'' collections. The myths found within ''[[Kojiki]]'' (712) are the oldest individual ones known to exist. The ''[[Nihon Ryōiki]]'' (c. 822) is the oldest ''setsuwa'' collection.
The ''setsuwa'' genre last until the early 14th century when it was succeeded by the ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]'' genre.

''Nihon Ryōiki'' was compiled in the early 9th century (Heian period) by the monk ''Kyōkai'', and contains 116 stories split between three volumes as well as nine poems. These stories originate both from older sources and from times contemporary to the compilation’s completion, and— like many Buddhist ''setsuwa''— are often focused on the concept of karmic retribution. No single complete manuscript of the ''Nihon Ryōiki'' exists— the complete text must be assembled from multiple sources.
''{{ill|Konjaku Monogatarishū|en|Konjaku Monogatarishū|WD=Q1507085|lt=Konjaku Monogatarishū|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū}}'' (Anthology of Tales from the Past) is a large compilation of disputed authorship (many suggest that the compilation was assembled by a Buddhist monk) comprising tales in the ''setsuwa'' style. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' was composed in Japan during the late Heian period, though the exact date of completion is unknown. ''Konjaku Monogatarishū'' contains over one thousand tales, and the works draw their content largely from folklore— both Buddhist and secular— from India, China, and Japan. The stories assembled contain relatively few references to Shinto and other non-Buddhist Japanese spirituality. The tales are not of great length, keeping with the style of ''setsuwa'' and its basis in oral tradition. The collection contains stories focusing on characters and happenings of many origins, including monks, peasantry, and nobility.
The {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} priest {{ill|Keisei (monk)|en|Keisei (monk)|WD=Q11495164|lt=Keisei|1=Keisei (monk)}} wrote ''Kankyo no Tomo'' in 1222.<ref name=RP>Rajyashree Pandey, "Women, Sexuality, and Enlightenment: ''Kankyo no Tomo''", ''Monumenta Nipponica'' '''50'''.3 (1995), pp. 325–356. {{JSTOR|2385548}}</ref>
